refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall update the worksite model to reflect current operational state (vehicle position, task progress, environmental changes, safety events) within p95 ≤ 1.0 s latency for all supported update scenarios (teleoperated commands, autonomous task execution, sensor data ingestion, safety alerts).
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-007 is a latency requirement that spans multiple operational modes and data sources. Refinement clarifies that 'worksite model' encompasses vehicle state, task state, and safety state; that latency is measured end-to-end from event occurrence to model reflection; and that the constraint applies across teleoperation, autonomous, and safety-critical paths. High safety criticality demands explicit inclusion of safety event propagation in the latency budget.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Instrument the system with timestamped event markers at source (sensor, command, alert generation), model update point, and consumer observation point. Measure p95 latency across representative scenarios: (1) teleoperated movement command → vehicle position update, (2) autonomous task execution → task progress update, (3) safety sensor trigger → safety alert in model. Validate against 1.0 s threshold under nominal and degraded network conditions.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-007.1: Acquire worksite state data (vehicle telemetry, task status, sensor readings, safety events) with sufficient frequency and fidelity.
FR-NGHE-007.2: Transmit worksite state updates from vehicle/field to central model with bounded network latency.
FR-NGHE-007.3: Process and integrate received state updates into the worksite model with bounded computation latency.
FR-NGHE-007.4: Distribute updated worksite model to authorized consumers (operators, safety systems, autonomous planners) with bounded delivery latency.
FR-NGHE-007.5: Prioritize safety-critical updates (safety alerts, emergency stops) to meet latency requirements even under congestion.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Centralized worksite model server with real-time telemetry ingestion pipeline, event-driven update propagation, and priority queuing for safety events.
DP-B: Distributed edge caching with local model replicas on vehicle and field gateways, eventual consistency synchronization, and safety event broadcast.
DP-C: Hybrid architecture: centralized authoritative model with edge-local caches, differential update protocol, and safety-critical fast-path bypass for alerts.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-C: Hybrid architecture with centralized authoritative worksite model, edge-local caches on vehicle and field gateways, differential update protocol (send only deltas), safety-critical fast-path for alerts, and priority queue for safety events. This balances latency (edge caching reduces round-trip delay), consistency (single source of truth), and safety (dedicated low-latency path for safety alerts).

potential_sources: Coupling between vehicle telemetry acquisition rate and network transmission frequency (mitigated by buffering and delta encoding).
Coupling between model update processing and consumer subscription management (mitigated by pub-sub decoupling).
Coupling between safety alert fast-path and normal update path (mitigated by separate priority queue and network channel).
coupling_check.notes: DP-C exhibits loose coupling via event-driven architecture and pub-sub messaging. Safety path is decoupled from nominal path to prevent congestion-induced latency inflation. Differential protocol reduces bandwidth coupling between update frequency and network capacity.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Network infrastructure supports priority queuing (QoS) for safety alerts. Risk: If network does not support QoS, safety alerts may be delayed by bulk telemetry. Mitigation: Validate network capability; implement application-level priority if needed.
Assumption: Edge gateways have sufficient compute and storage for local model caching. Risk: Undersized gateways may become bottleneck. Mitigation: Capacity planning and load testing.
Assumption: Differential update protocol can be implemented with acceptable overhead. Risk: Delta computation and encoding may add latency. Mitigation: Prototype and benchmark.

Assumption: Safety alert fast-path does not require full model consistency. Risk: Operators may act on stale model while receiving fresh alerts. Mitigation: Design safety alerts to be self-contained; include relevant context in alert payload.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-C to explicitly specify: (1) maximum acceptable latency budget allocation across acquisition, transmission, processing, and delivery stages; (2) network QoS requirements (e.g., priority levels, bandwidth reservation); (3) edge cache consistency model (e.g., eventual consistency with bounded staleness); (4) safety alert payload content to ensure operators have sufficient context without requiring full model lookup.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: The NGHE Solution is the top-level system boundary encompassing all subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) and their integration. At this decomposition level, the worksite model update latency requirement is assigned to NGHE Solution as the system-level owner, with realization distributed across Vehicle (telemetry acquisition), Teleoperation (network transmission and model processing), and Safety Services (alert prioritization and distribution).

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle and field sensors transmit telemetry (position, orientation, task status, sensor readings) to the worksite model. Frequency and latency of telemetry ingestion directly impact model update latency.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Operators and autonomous planners issue task commands (move, dig, load) and receive task status updates. Task execution state must be reflected in worksite model within latency budget.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Safety Services generate and distribute safety alerts (collision warning, stability alert, emergency stop) to operators and autonomous systems. Safety alerts must propagate through worksite model with highest priority to meet latency requirement.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Communication channel between vehicle/field and central worksite model. Network latency, bandwidth, and reliability are critical to overall update latency. QoS and priority queuing on Network Link are essential for meeting latency budget.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the system-level owner of the worksite model update latency requirement. All sub-components (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) contribute to meeting this requirement. Containment is high because the requirement is system-level and NGHE Solution is the integration point. Minimal diffusion because the requirement is clearly scoped to worksite model updates.

Vehicle is responsible for telemetry acquisition and transmission to the worksite model. However, Vehicle does not own the central model or the distribution to consumers. Containment is moderate because Vehicle owns the first stage (acquisition) but not the full end-to-end latency path. Diffusion is significant because latency depends on network and processing outside Vehicle.

Teleoperation owns the central worksite model, network transmission, and model processing/distribution. This is the primary realization point for the latency requirement. Containment is high because Teleoperation controls the critical path (model update and distribution). Diffusion is moderate because latency also depends on Vehicle telemetry frequency and Safety Services alert prioritization.

Safety Services is responsible for safety alert generation and prioritization within the worksite model update path. Safety alerts must meet the latency requirement, but Safety Services does not own the full end-to-end path. Containment is moderate because Safety Services owns alert prioritization but not the underlying model or network. Diffusion is high because safety alert latency depends on model processing and network QoS.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Teleoperation is the primary owner of the worksite model update latency requirement because it owns the central worksite model, network transmission protocol, and model processing/distribution logic. Vehicle contributes telemetry acquisition, and Safety Services contributes alert prioritization, but Teleoperation integrates these inputs and is responsible for end-to-end latency performance. Ownership of Teleoperation ensures accountability for the latency budget allocation across acquisition, transmission, processing, and delivery stages.
containment_risks: Risk: Teleoperation may not have direct control over Vehicle telemetry acquisition frequency. Mitigation: Establish telemetry frequency requirements in Vehicle interface specification.
Risk: Teleoperation may not have direct control over Network Link QoS. Mitigation: Establish network QoS requirements in Network Link interface specification; validate with network provider.

Risk: Safety Services alert generation latency may not be visible to Teleoperation. Mitigation: Establish alert generation latency requirements in Safety Services interface specification; instrument and monitor.

Risk: Distributed edge caches (on Vehicle and field gateways) may introduce consistency issues that impact latency. Mitigation: Define cache consistency model and synchronization protocol; validate with simulation and testing.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Refine IM assignment to explicitly allocate latency budget across sub-components: (1) Vehicle: telemetry acquisition and transmission ≤ 200 ms; (2) Network Link: transmission ≤ 300 ms (p95); (3) Teleoperation: model processing and distribution ≤ 400 ms; (4) Safety Services: alert generation and prioritization ≤ 100 ms. Establish interface specifications for Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, and Network Link that include latency requirements and measurement points. Assign ownership of latency budget tracking and performance monitoring to Teleoperation with visibility into Vehicle and Safety Services contributions.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-C (hybrid architecture with centralized model, edge caching, differential updates, and safety fast-path) directly addresses the refined FR requirement for p95 ≤ 1.0 s latency across all update scenarios. The DP is logically sound: edge caching reduces round-trip latency, differential protocol reduces bandwidth and processing overhead, safety fast-path ensures alert prioritization, and pub-sub decoupling prevents congestion-induced delays. No independence axiom violations detected; the DP is a coherent solution to the FR.

diffusion_risks: Diffusion Risk 1: Telemetry acquisition frequency is owned by Vehicle but impacts Teleoperation's model update latency. Mitigation: Establish telemetry frequency SLA in Vehicle interface.
Diffusion Risk 2: Network QoS is owned by infrastructure but impacts Teleoperation's transmission latency. Mitigation: Establish network QoS requirements; validate with network provider.

Diffusion Risk 3: Safety alert generation latency is owned by Safety Services but impacts overall worksite model update latency. Mitigation: Establish alert generation latency SLA in Safety Services interface.
Diffusion Risk 4: Edge cache consistency is distributed across Vehicle and field gateways but impacts model accuracy and latency. Mitigation: Define cache consistency model; validate with simulation.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation is assigned as the primary owner of the worksite model update latency requirement. This is appropriate because Teleoperation owns the central model, network transmission, and model processing/distribution. However, the requirement is inherently distributed: Vehicle contributes telemetry, Network Link contributes transmission, and Safety Services contributes alert prioritization. Teleoperation must establish clear interface specifications and SLAs with these contributors to ensure end-to-end latency performance. The hybrid architecture (centralized model with edge caching) introduces additional complexity: edge caches on Vehicle and field gateways must be kept consistent with the central model, and this synchronization overhead must be accounted for in the latency budget.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible via instrumentation and testing. Timestamp event markers at source (sensor, command, alert generation), model update point, and consumer observation point. Measure p95 latency across representative scenarios in lab and field environments. Validate against 1.0 s threshold under nominal and degraded network conditions. Automated monitoring and alerting can track latency performance in production.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear at the system level (NGHE Solution owns the requirement) and at the subsystem level (Teleoperation is the primary owner with contributions from Vehicle, Network Link, and Safety Services). However, the latency budget allocation across sub-components must be refined: specify maximum acceptable latency for each stage (acquisition, transmission, processing, delivery) and for each contributor (Vehicle, Network Link, Teleoperation, Safety Services). This will enable clear accountability and enable early detection of latency issues.

recommended_revisions: Refine latency budget allocation: Vehicle ≤ 200 ms, Network Link ≤ 300 ms (p95), Teleoperation ≤ 400 ms, Safety Services ≤ 100 ms. Total: 1.0 s.
Establish interface specifications for Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, and Network Link that include latency requirements, measurement points, and SLAs.
Define edge cache consistency model and synchronization protocol; validate with simulation and testing.
Establish monitoring and alerting for latency performance; track p95 latency across all update scenarios in production.
Conduct network QoS validation with network provider; ensure priority queuing and bandwidth reservation are available for safety alerts.

decision.justification: The refined FR is not atomic and has been decomposed into five sub-FRs (acquisition, transmission, processing, distribution, prioritization). Each sub-FR requires further decomposition to define specific design parameters and implementation modules. The selected DP (hybrid architecture) is logically sound but requires detailed design of the differential update protocol, edge cache consistency model, and safety alert fast-path. The assigned IM (Teleoperation as primary owner) is appropriate but requires explicit latency budget allocation and interface specifications. Proceeding to the next decomposition level will enable detailed design of each sub-FR and validation of the latency budget allocation.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-007.1: Acquire worksite state data (vehicle telemetry, task status, sensor readings, safety events) with sufficient frequency and fidelity.
FR-NGHE-007.2: Transmit worksite state updates from vehicle/field to central model with bounded network latency.
FR-NGHE-007.3: Process and integrate received state updates into the worksite model with bounded computation latency.
FR-NGHE-007.4: Distribute updated worksite model to authorized consumers (operators, safety systems, autonomous planners) with bounded delivery latency.
FR-NGHE-007.5: Prioritize safety-critical updates (safety alerts, emergency stops) to meet latency requirements even under congestion.

assumptions: Network infrastructure supports QoS and priority queuing for safety alerts.
Edge gateways (on Vehicle and field) have sufficient compute and storage for local model caching.
Differential update protocol can be implemented with acceptable overhead.
Safety alert fast-path does not require full model consistency; alerts are self-contained with sufficient context.
Teleoperation has visibility into Vehicle telemetry frequency and Safety Services alert generation latency.
Latency budget allocation across sub-components is feasible and can be validated through testing.
open_risks: Network QoS may not be available or may be degraded under high load; safety alerts may be delayed.
Edge cache consistency may introduce latency overhead or staleness issues.
Differential update protocol may add latency due to delta computation and encoding.
Safety alert generation latency may exceed allocated budget if Safety Services is not optimized.
Vehicle telemetry acquisition frequency may be insufficient to meet model update latency requirement.
Distributed ownership of latency budget may lead to accountability gaps and performance issues.
evidence_needed: Network QoS capability assessment and validation with network provider.
Edge gateway capacity analysis and load testing.
Differential update protocol prototype and latency benchmark.
Safety alert generation latency measurement and optimization.
Vehicle telemetry acquisition frequency analysis and optimization.
End-to-end latency measurement across representative scenarios in lab and field environments.
Simulation and testing of edge cache consistency model.
Production monitoring and alerting for latency performance.
